sources.notes: Read the complete operation definition, examples, and constraints: sum(n) <= 2e5, a_i <= 1e18, and b_i >= 2.
Official editorial confirms Cartesian-tree interval normalization, the operation-count/minimum-maximum tree DP, the 63-state bound, and the linear merge of monotone min-max convolution states.
Read the verifier-authored solution and code; it proves operation-order independence and implements the direct O(K^2) child merge, including the a_i-1 floor-division representation.
Independent contest write-up cross-checks the Cartesian-tree DP and gives the exact two-pointer rule: spend the next operation on the child whose current maximum is larger.

Re-proved ceiling-division commutativity and the monotone min-max merge; exhaustive BFS matched both DP merges on 111150 compact instances, and 100000 random monotone-curve merges matched quadratic convolution.

annotation.summary: Two positive arrays a and b are given. One operation chooses a segment, lets x be the minimum b-value on it, and replaces every covered a_i by ceil(a_i/x). Find the minimum operations needed to make every a_i equal to 1.
annotation.constraints: There are up to 1e4 tests; 1 <= n <= 2e5 per test and sum(n) <= 2e5; 1 <= a_i <= 1e18 and 2 <= b_i <= 1e18. Values require 64-bit arithmetic. Repeating the whole-array operation needs at most 60 divisions, so a conservative state cap K=63 is sufficient.
annotation.core_idea: For positive p and q, ceil(ceil(x/p)/q)=ceil(x/(pq)), so operations affecting the same position commute and may be reordered. For any chosen segment, take its leftmost minimum position u and expand the segment as far as possible while preserving b_u as that tie-broken minimum; the divisor is unchanged and covering more positions cannot hurt. With the stable tie rule, these maximal intervals are exactly the subtrees of the min Cartesian tree of b. Let f_u[k] be the minimum possible maximum current a-value in u's subtree after exactly k operations wholly inside that subtree. Merge the child curves by base_u[k]=min_{i+j=k} max(f_left[i],f_right[j],a_u), with the evident one-child cases, then account for using u's whole subtree interval by f_u[0]=base_u[0] and f_u[k]=min(base_u[k],ceil(f_u[k-1]/b_u)). Every curve is non-increasing. Therefore the two-child min-max convolution is computed in O(K): start i=j=0, output max(f_left[i],f_right[j]) for total k=i+j, and advance the pointer whose current value is larger, since advancing the non-bottleneck side cannot improve the maximum. Build the Cartesian tree with a monotonic stack and process it bottom-up; the answer is the first k with f_root[k]=1.
annotation.complexity: O(n log(max a_i)) time and O(n log(max a_i)) memory with K=63 and the monotone min-max merge; the direct merge variant is O(n log^2(max a_i)) time with the same memory.
tricks: Choose the leftmost occurrence of a segment minimum to make equal b-values obey one stable Cartesian-tree tie convention.
Expand every operation to its canonical maximal interval because the divisor stays fixed and extra covered positions cannot be harmed.
Use ceil(ceil(x/p)/q)=ceil(x/(pq)) to reorder all child operations before ancestor-interval operations.
Swap the objective and budget: store the smallest achievable subtree maximum for each operation count instead of storing a huge numeric value as a state.
Cap the operation-count dimension at 63 because the global minimum b is at least 2.
Merge two non-increasing DP curves by always advancing the side that currently realizes the bottleneck maximum.
Optionally replace each a_i by a_i-1 so every ceiling division becomes ordinary floor division and the target becomes zero.
verification_notes: Exhaustive shortest-path search over all 111150 instances with 1 <= n <= 4, a_i in [1,6], and b_i in [2,4] matched both the quadratic and optimized Cartesian-tree DP, including duplicate b-values.
On 100000 random pairs of non-increasing curves of lengths up to 20, the bottleneck-advancing merge produced exactly the same convolution as enumerating every split.

solution_variants.name: cartesian-tree-dp-monotone-minmax-merge
solution_variants.summary: Build the stable min Cartesian tree of b. In postorder, keep the K+1 operation-count/minimum-maximum states, merge two child curves along the bottleneck-advancing two-pointer path, include a_u, and relax repeated whole-subtree divisions by b_u.
solution_variants.complexity: O(n log(max a_i)) time and O(n log(max a_i)) memory; concretely O(63n) for the stated limits.

solution_variants.name: cartesian-tree-dp-direct-knapsack-merge
solution_variants.summary: Use the same canonical intervals and tree-DP state, but at each node with two children enumerate every split i+j=k to compute the min-max merge. This is the simpler verifier-solution implementation and retains the same 63-state cap.
solution_variants.complexity: O(n log^2(max a_i)) time and O(n log(max a_i)) memory; concretely O(63^2 n) in the worst-case bound.

tags.evidence: Every useful operation is normalized to a maximal interval with one tie-broken minimum b_u, and those laminar intervals are exactly the subtrees of the min Cartesian tree on b.
The solution computes f_u[k] bottom-up for each Cartesian-tree subtree, combines the two child subproblems, and then applies the transition belonging to node u's whole subtree.

tags.description: Dynamic-programming merges of the form h[k]=min_{i+j=k} max(f[i],g[j]) over a bottleneck objective; use when recognizing or optimizing this convolution is a central reusable step, not for arbitrary transitions that merely contain min or max.
tags.created_reason: Existing dp/knapsack-dp and trick/monotonicity do not identify the reusable bottleneck convolution at the center of CF2048F. The official optimized solution explicitly treats the child merge as min-max convolution and reduces it from quadratic to linear time for monotone curves.
tags.evidence: For each total child-operation budget k, the merge is exactly min over i+j=k of max(f_left[i],f_right[j]); evaluating all these bottleneck states is the dominant DP transition.

Both child DP curves are non-increasing in their operation counts, so only reducing the currently larger value can lower the bottleneck; this monotonicity collapses each quadratic min-max merge to one O(K) path.
The optimized merge maintains one operation-count pointer per child and advances the pointer on the side with the larger current DP value.
A monotonic stack constructs the stable min Cartesian tree of b in O(n), including a consistent strict-comparison rule for equal values.
The simpler documented variant distributes a fixed operation budget k between the two child subtrees by enumerating every split i+j=k, which is a tree-knapsack merge.
